questions.text: Select the option which fits appropriately in the blank:_ icon like this is called _ hourglass.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: An, A — It is wrong because "a" can't be used before "hourglass" (silent 'h').
An, The — "The" would refer to a specific hourglass, but here it's a general statement about what it's called.

This is the correct answer, here's why;Before "icon":"Icon" starts with a vowel sound (i), so we use "an"."An icon like this..."Before "hourglass":Although "hourglass" starts with "h", the 'h' is silent, so it sounds like it starts with a vowel sound (our-glass).So, we use "an" before "hourglass".
The, An — Wrong because "the icon" would refer to a specific one already mentioned, but it's introduced for the first time.

questions.explanation: "An icon like this is called an hourglass."Here's why each article is used:"An" before "icon": The indefinite article "an" is used before "icon" because "icon" starts with a vowel sound."An" before "hourglass": The indefinite article "an" is used before "hourglass" for the same reason — "hourglass" also starts with a vowel sound.

questions.subject: English

options.explanation: The given sentence is a simple present tense. As per grammar rules, we use the first form of the verb in simple tense, so option A is the right choice here. So the correct sentence will be:Bilal lives with his brother.
Is living is showing the present continuous tense. A present continuous tense denotes actions/events that are already in progress while we speak about them.
Has lived is in the present perfect tense because of the helping verb 'has'
Had lived here is pointing towards the past perfect tense.

questions.explanation: The simple present or present indefinite is one of the verb forms associated with the present tense in modern English. It is commonly referred to as a tense, although it also encodes certain information about aspect in addition to present time. The given sentence is a simple present tense. As per Grammar rules, we use the first form of the verb in simple tense, so option A is the right choice here. So our correct sentence will be: Bilal lives with his brother.

questions.text: In the following question, four alternative sentences are given. Choose the correct option.
questions.subject: English

options.text: E-mail is a relatively new mean of communication.
options.explanation: The word "mean" should be plural, which should be "means."

options.text: E-mail is a relatively new mean to communication.
options.explanation: "Means" should be used instead of "mean." Additionally, the preposition "to" should be "of" to correctly express the idea of communication.

options.text: E-mail is a relatively new means of communication.
options.explanation: The correct form is "means of communication" because it refers to the method or way by which communication is carried out, in this case, through e-mail. The term "means" is plural to indicate that there are multiple ways or methods available for communication.It's important to note that the correct option also includes the word "relatively" to indicate that e-mail is a comparatively new method of communication, implying that it has not been in existence for an extensive period of time.

options.text: E-mail is relatively new means to communication.
options.explanation: The preposition "to" should be "of" to correctly express the idea of communication. Article "a" should be used before relatively to express the correct idea of communication.

questions.explanation: Options A and B are wrong because they use “mean” which is not an appropriate word here it should be “means” because “means” implies “a system to convey or a way to do something” while “mean” implies “signify/denote or to aim/intend” something.Option D is wrong because it does not use the article “a” before relatively, which makes the sentence incomplete. Secondly, there is always “means of” something like “way of doing something”, never “means to” do something.

questions.text: Fill in the blank with the correct option:None of them _ able to solve this question.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This option is correct because "none" is a singular pronoun that takes a singular verb, and "were" is the correct singular past tense of the verb "to be".- "Were" agrees with the singular subject "none" and indicates the past tense, which fits the context of the sentence.
This option is incorrect because "none" is a singular pronoun that takes a singular verb, but "are" is a plural verb.
None of them means not even a single person so it is singular. 2) Them is plural but since none of them is given, the answer should was

This option uses the conditional tense form of the verb "be", which indicates a hypothetical or uncertain situation, and doesn't fit the context of describing a current inability to solve the question. Incorrect

questions.explanation: None of them means not even a single person so it is singular. 2) Them is plural but since none of them is given, the answer should was

questions.text: She will _ her lesson for two months next week.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Has learnt is in the refers to an action in the past and so it is incorrect.
The sentence is in the present continuous tense. And so she has been learning makes sense when read out aloud but more importantly, it is grammatically incorrect. So option C is the correct option.
'Have been learning' incorrectly uses the present perfect continuous tense, indicating an ongoing action that will continue into the future.
'Has been learns' is grammatically incorrect because 'learns' cannot follow 'has been'. The correct form is 'has been learning'.

questions.explanation: The sentence refers to an action that started in the past and will continue into the future, next week, requiring the present perfect continuous tense. The correct form is 'has been learning', as it matches the singular subject 'She' and the future context.'Has learnt' is incorrect due to its past perfect tense, not aligning with the future context. 'Have been learning' is incorrect because 'have' does not agree with the singular subject 'She'. 'Has been learns' is grammatically incorrect, as 'learns' cannot be used in this tense structure.

questions.text: Choose the correct sentence from the following options:
questions.subject: English

options.text: Last year, Matt earned twice as much as his brother, who has a better position.
options.explanation: The sentence is grammatically correct because the clause “who has a better position” properly modifies “his brother.” It clearly contrasts last year’s earnings with the brother’s current job status.

options.text: Last year, Matt earn twice more than his brother, who has a better position.
options.explanation: The sentence is written in past tense because of the use of last year. Hence, earned would be used here.The sentence provided contains a grammatical error. Instead of saying "twice more," you should use "twice as much."

options.text: Last year, Matt earned twice as many as his brother, who had a better position.
options.explanation: As much is the better option relative to as many as this refers to more than two people being compared. the revised sentence is still not correct. When comparing earnings, it is more appropriate to use "twice as much" rather than "twice as many."

options.text: Last year, Matt earned twice as more as his brother, which has a better position.
options.explanation: When referring to a person or a pet, the pronoun ‘who’ is used instead of ‘which’. the sentence is still not correct. Instead of saying "twice as more" you should use "twice as much."

questions.explanation: Since the sentence is in the past tense, hence the verb used will be earned.As much is the better option relative to as many as this refers to more than 2 people being compared. Who is the right pronoun when one refers to a person.
